fr:title.text: Pseudocommutativity and lax idempotency for relative pseudomonads

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pseudocommutativity and lax idempotency for relative pseudomonads

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Includes, among other things, a definition of strong (relative) monad on a multicategories.

A (left) strength of a monad T with respect to a monoidal structure \otimes is a natural transformation \sigma : \Gamma \otimes T A \to T (\Gamma \otimes A) satisfying certain laws. A concise formulation is that a left-strength is a lifting of T from a monad on categories to a monad on actegories, with the monoidal category \mathcal C acting on itself. If \mathcal C is a monoidal closed category, then strength is equivalently an enrichment of T.

The most general version of this is given by Slattery: a strong monad on multicategories is given by a unit map \mathcal C(X;T X) and an extension map \mathcal C(\Gamma ,X,\Delta ;TY) \to \mathcal C(\Gamma ,TX,\Delta ;TY) satisfying some laws. This makes the connection to the semantics of effectful programming languages apparent: a monad only has an extension map for unary morphisms but a strength allows for an extension of any single input of a multimorphism. In programming languages terms, the strength is what allows for the use of a monadic bind in the presence of other variables.
Despite the common term strong monad, a strength is in general a structure, though in some special cases it is a property.
All monads on the category of sets are canonically Set-enriched and therefore carry a strength. Non-trivial strengths abound in (pre)sheaf semantics.
